ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general octopus food product class (FOODON_03302051); no specific entry for battered or karaage-style fried octopus exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked octopus generically; no karaage-specific or battered-fried octopus code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried octopus or karaage octopus as a food substance.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Tako Karaage is approximately 180 g, comprising several pieces of deep-fried baby octopus tentacles accompanied by garlic cream cheese whip and ikura (salmon roe) garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Octopus, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15166); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried battered seafood coating and cream cheese to account for frying and accompaniments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Tako Karaage exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked octopus (NDB 15166) with adjustments for deep-frying in a starch-flour batter (increased fat and carbohydrate) and a minor contribution from cream cheese and ikura accompaniments. The composite reflects a typical restaurant preparation. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on battered fried seafood and starch-coated fried foods (GI ~40–50)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Tako Karaage or fried octopus exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 45 based on analogous battered and deep-fried seafood products (GI range 40–55) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The starch-flour batter contributes moderate glycemic carbohydrates; the protein and fat content of octopus suppresses the overall GI. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 12.5 × 1.80) / 100 = 10.1 g, using 180 g serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~756 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Tako Karaage to occasional consumption, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 756 mg sodium — roughly one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from soy-based marinade and seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from starch-flour batter and estimated glycemic index of 45
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as the starch-flour batter contributes approximately 22.5 g of carbohydrate per 180 g serving with an estimated glycemic load of 10, which is moderate and can affect postprandial blood glucose; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting portion to half a serving is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; cholesterol from octopus and ikura
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume Tako Karaage sparingly, as deep-frying raises total fat to approximately 9.8 g per 100 g with 2.4 g saturated fat, and the ikura garnish adds dietary cholesterol; the omega-3 content of octopus provides a partial cardiovascular benefit but does not offset the overall fat profile of the fried preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tako Karaage (タコの唐揚げ) is a Japanese deep-fried octopus dish in which baby octopus tentacles are marinated — typically in soy sauce, sake, mirin, and sesame oil — then coated in a light batter of wheat flour and potato starch before being deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining tender and juicy inside. The dish is a popular izakaya (Japanese gastropub) appetiser and has been adopted widely in Japanese-influenced restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The version served at The Oyster Bank (Funan, Singapore) features garlic cream cheese whip and ikura (cured salmon roe), which add richness, umami, and a briny pop of flavour. Nutritionally, Tako Karaage is a moderate-energy food (~195 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~14.5 g per 100 g) from octopus, which is also a notable source of vitamin B12, selenium, and iron. The deep-frying process and cream cheese accompaniment elevate fat content relative to plain cooked octopus. Sodium is elevated due to soy-based marinade. The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low), making the carbohydrate load moderate per serving. Clinically, the dish warrants attention for sodium content in hypertensive individuals and for carbohydrate and fat load in those managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tako karaage?
Is tako karaage high in sodium?
What does tako karaage taste like?
What allergens are in tako karaage?

ingredient_synonyms.karaage: Japanese-style deep-frying technique using starch-flour batter
ingredient_synonyms.garlic cream cheese whip: whipped cream cheese with garlic, garlic aioli variant

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Tako Karaage exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked octopus data combined with adjustments for deep-frying batter, cream cheese, and ikura. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from USDA is well-established and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15166, octopus cooked moist heat) with upward adjustments to fat_g and carbohydrate_g to account for deep-frying in a starch-flour batter and minor contributions from cream cheese and ikura accompaniments. No direct approved-source entry for Tako Karaage exists. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy octopus entry and is not reliably estimated for this composite preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy octopus data and are approximate. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried seafood products in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition); gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 5. Halal status is marked false as the dish is served at a restaurant that also serves alcoholic beverages and the marinade may contain mirin (alcohol-containing); halal certification is not confirmed. 6. The cuisine_tag 'Alcoholic Drink' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform categorisation artefact and does not reflect the food category of Tako Karaage; it has been disregarded. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A0CJW are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for karaage-style fried octopus exists in either system.
